planting.note: Plant offshoots ('pups') with attached roots in rich, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; each stem fruits once then dies, but the clump produces a continuous succession of pups. Plant in a protected spot sheltered from strong winds, which shred the large leaves.

directions: Plant pups in rich, moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; banana is the most-consumed fruit on earth and one of the most productive food plants for Gulf Coast zone 8–11 gardens, where established clumps produce a nearly continuous succession of hands. Each pseudostem flowers once and fruits once, then should be cut to the ground after harvest, allowing the pups already pushing up around it to take over. In zone 8–9 the top growth dies back in hard freezes but established root mats resprout reliably in spring; heavy mulching protects the rhizomes. Popular zone-8-hardy varieties include 'Orinoco,' 'Lady Finger,' 'Dwarf Cavendish,' and the ultra-cold-hardy ornamental/edible 'Basjoo' hybrid. The large, exotic leaves make banana a spectacular tropical accent plant even in years when the winter prevents fruiting.

planting.note: Twist the crown from a store-bought pineapple, allow the cut end to dry 2–3 days, then plant 2 inches deep in very well-drained, sandy, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0) soil in full sun; slips from the sides of the fruit root fastest and fruit soonest. In zone 9a grow in a large container and protect from frost.

directions: Plant crowns or slips in very well-drained, sandy, acidic soil in full sun; pineapple is a bromeliad that grows terrestrially, producing a rosette of stiff, spiny-edged leaves from which a single fruit-bearing stalk emerges from the center. It is surprisingly easy to grow from the crown of a grocery store fruit — simply twist it off, dry the base for a few days, and plant in sandy, well-drained soil. Fruit matures in 18–24 months from crown planting, or 12–15 months from slips (the small plantlets that grow along the fruit stem). After fruiting, cut the old plant but leave the ratoon shoots that sprout from the base — these produce a second and often third crop in less time than starting over. In zone 9a, grow in a large container in full sun and bring indoors or to a greenhouse when frost threatens; pineapple is frost-sensitive at 28°F.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; Barbados cherry is one of the easiest tropical fruits for zone 9–11 and fruits multiple times per year in warm climates. It is sensitive to freezing and needs protection below 28°F.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; Barbados cherry (acerola) is one of the richest sources of vitamin C of any plant on Earth — the small red fruits contain 65 times more vitamin C per 100g than an orange, making even a few fruits a valuable daily supplement. The plant is a dense, attractive evergreen shrub with small, shiny leaves and clusters of small pink flowers that appear 3–4 times per year in zone 9–11, followed quickly by ripening cherry-like fruits. Berries ripen to bright red in 25 days after flowering and must be harvested immediately — they are extremely perishable at room temperature. Use fresh, blended, or frozen immediately after harvest. The shrub makes an excellent fruiting hedge in frost-free gardens; in zone 9a it is best grown in a container or in a protected south-facing microclimate.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; hyacinth bean is a fast-growing vine requiring a sturdy trellis, fence, or arbor — it can reach 10–15 feet in a single season. Soak seeds in water 8–12 hours before planting to speed germination.

directions: Direct sow after last frost in full sun on a sturdy trellis or fence; hyacinth bean is one of the most ornamental edible vines available — the twining stems, deep purple-flushed leaves, spikes of purple or white flowers, and gleaming metallic-purple flat pods in late summer and fall make it as decorative as any flowering vine while also producing edible beans and pods. The immature green pods are used like snap beans; mature beans must be thoroughly cooked (raw beans contain hydrocyanic acid). In zone 9–11 it is perennial and grows as a woody vine; elsewhere treat as a vigorous warm-season annual that can cover a large arbor in weeks. One of the fastest-growing summer vines for a quick privacy screen or shade structure. Thomas Jefferson grew it at Monticello.

planting.note: Direct sow 1–2 inches deep after danger of hard frost in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in full sun; scarlet runner bean prefers cooler temperatures than most beans and slows or stops flowering in summer heat above 80°F — in zone 9a it is best grown in spring and fall. A sturdy trellis 5–6 feet tall is required.

directions: Direct sow after frost danger in fertile, moist soil on a tall trellis in full sun; scarlet runner bean is a dual-purpose ornamental and edible vine native to the mountains of Central America, producing brilliant scarlet (occasionally white or bicolor) flowers beloved by hummingbirds throughout the growing season, followed by long flat pods with striking maroon-speckled white seeds. Pods are harvested young as snap beans (very similar to pole beans in texture and flavor) or allowed to mature for the attractive dried beans used in soups and stews. The plant is a perennial in zones 7–11, where established tuberous roots resprout each season; in cooler zones treat as an annual. In zone 9a spring plantings often stop producing in summer heat — plant a second succession in late August for fall harvest.

planting.note: Nick or file the hard seed coat until the white inner surface is visible, then soak in warm water (changed daily) until the seed swells and a sprout emerges; transplant to a wide, shallow container of heavy clay garden soil without drainage holes, submerged 2–6 inches below the water surface in full sun.

directions: Nick the hard seed coat and soak in warm water until sprouting; transplant to a wide, shallow tub of heavy clay soil submerged 2–6 inches below the water surface in full sun — sacred lotus is one of the most magnificent aquatic plants on Earth and one of the most culturally significant plants in human history, sacred in Hinduism, Buddhism, and ancient Egyptian tradition. The enormous, water-repelling leaves (2 feet across) on tall stems and fragrant, luminous pink or white flowers up to 12 inches wide rising 4–6 feet above the water are incomparably beautiful. Every part is edible: the crunchy, lace-patterned rhizomes (lotus root) are a prized vegetable in Asian cuisine, the seeds are eaten raw when young or dried and roasted, the large leaves are used to wrap food for steaming. The lotus is winter-hardy in zone 5–10 if the rhizomes are protected from freeze. It grows best in a large (15+ gallon) container sunk in a pond or placed in full sun.

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in fall in extremely well-drained, acidic to neutral, lean soil in full sun to partial shade; strawberry tree is highly drought tolerant once established and actually performs better in poor, infertile soil — rich, fertile soil promotes lush growth that is more susceptible to diseases.

directions: Plant in poor, very well-drained, acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; the strawberry tree is a beautiful, slow-growing Mediterranean evergreen that produces clusters of small white urn-shaped flowers in fall and winter simultaneously with the ripening of the previous year's strawberry-like red and orange fruits — a unique garden spectacle of flowers and ripe fruit coexisting on the tree. The rough-textured red fruits are edible but taste nothing like a strawberry: they are mealy, mildly sweet, with a somewhat gritty texture, best used for jams, jellies, and wine rather than eating fresh (the name refers only to their appearance). The bark is a beautiful cinnamon red and exfoliating. The dwarf cultivar 'Compacta' stays 5–6 feet; 'Elfin King' is an ultra-compact form. Excellent drought-tolerant, deer-resistant evergreen for zone 7–10.

common_name: Kirengeshoma (Japanese Wax Bells, Yellow Wax Bells)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in consistently moist, deeply humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–6.0) soil in partial to full shade; kirengeshoma is demanding about soil moisture and acidity — it will not tolerate dry or alkaline conditions and wilts dramatically between watering in summer heat.

directions: Plant in consistently moist, deeply humus-rich, strongly acidic soil in partial to full shade; kirengeshoma is one of the most refined and unusual of all shade garden perennials — a tall, graceful Japanese woodland plant with majestic, deeply lobed, maple-like leaves on arching dark stems, followed by nodding clusters of pendulous, pale yellow, waxy tubular bells in late summer and fall, a season when few other shade plants flower. The flowers last for weeks and have a luminous, translucent quality. It is an outstanding late-season bloomer that fills the gap when hostas and ferns have finished. It requires acidic, consistently moist woodland soil and protection from afternoon sun and summer drought — mulch deeply and water regularly in summer. Combines superbly with late-season hostas, asters, and Japanese anemone in the shade garden.

planting.note: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; rose apple grows quickly and begins fruiting within 3–4 years. It tolerates brief flooding and grows naturally along stream banks. Fruit from a single tree requires no pollinator.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic soil in full sun; rose apple is a medium-sized tropical evergreen tree native to Southeast Asia with spectacular, showy white flowers that look like giant powder puffs (from long prominent stamens) and distinctive fruit — pale yellow to pink, waxy, bell-shaped, 1–2 inches across, with thin crisp flesh and a delicate, unmistakable rose-water flavor and aroma. The scent of the ripe fruit is truly rose-like and makes it one of the most aromatic tropical fruits. The crisp, refreshing flesh is eaten fresh, added to fruit salads, or made into rose-flavored syrup and jelly. The tree forms a broad, dense crown with pendulous branches and is an excellent ornamental shade tree for zone 10–11 landscapes. It is considered invasive in Hawaii and some Pacific islands — plant responsibly and do not plant near natural areas.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown plants in well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; annatto is drought tolerant once established and grows quickly in warm, humid conditions. It is frost-sensitive and suited to outdoor culture only in frost-free zone 10–12.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; annatto (achiote) is a tropical American shrub known worldwide as the natural source of the orange-red pigment used to color cheese, butter, smoked fish, and countless processed foods — the intensely colored seeds inside the spiny red seed pods are ground or infused in oil to make the widely used Latin American spice paste. The seeds color and flavor rice, tamales, soups, and sauces in Latin American and Filipino cuisine. The attractive plant also produces large pink-lavender flowers similar to single roses followed by striking, bristly bright-red seed pods in clusters. A striking ornamental and useful spice shrub for zone 10–12 gardens; in zone 9a it may be grown in a large container and protected from frost.
